{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl/issues/111","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl/issues/111/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl/issues/111/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl/issues/111/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl/issues/111","id":2705045735,"node_id":"I_kwDOKdArvc6hO7jn","number":111,"title":"Error if there is an space in `sshflags`  "-i path_to_ssh_key"","user":{"login":"davidbp","id":4223580,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQyMjM1ODA=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4223580?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidbp","html_url":"https://github.com/davidbp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidbp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidbp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidbp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidbp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidbp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidbp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidbp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidbp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidbp/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-11-29T12:58:12Z","updated_at":"2024-12-02T18:52:09Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hi 

I was trying to execute the following 

```julia
procs = addprocs([("dbp@ip:port", 4)], 
    dir="/home/dbp", 
    exename="/home/dbp/julia/bin/julia", 
    sshflags="-i path_to_ssh_key",
    tunnel=true,
)
```

but could not make it work. I was getting an error with message `path_to_ssh_key not accessible`, after asking in Discourse it was mentioned  to try  `sshflags="-ipath_to_ssh_key"` (without the space between -i and the path) and it worked.

In the documentation it is mentioned with an space

```
    •  sshflags: specifies additional ssh options, e.g. sshflags=`-i /home/foo/bar.pem`
```
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl/issues/111/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl/issues/111/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl/issues/comments/2512435889","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl/issues/111#issuecomment-2512435889","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl/issues/111","id":2512435889,"node_id":"IC_kwDOKdArvc6VwLqx","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-02T18:52:09Z","updated_at":"2024-12-02T18:52:09Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Try
```
procs = addprocs([("dbp@ip:port", 4)], 
    dir="/home/dbp", 
    exename="/home/dbp/julia/bin/julia", 
    sshflags=`-i path_to_ssh_key`,
    tunnel=true,
)
```
as the [documentation](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/stdlib/Distributed/#Distributed.addprocs) says. The grave accents (aka backticks) will create a [Cmd](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/base/#Base.Cmd) object, which is Julia's way to represent command-line arguments (which is internally a kind of string vector similar to `["-i", "path_to_ssh_key"]`).

If you use normal String quotes, you are passing a single ARGV element "-i path_to_ssh_key" to ssh, as if you had written `ssh "-i path_to_ssh_key"` on the command line, which is probably not what you wanted and which SSH won't understand.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Distributed.jl/issues/comments/2512435889/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

That makes it desirable to *not* require any prefix for filenames on the command line, IMHO.

Try (e.g. in zsh):
```
% touch test-file
% julia --trace-compile test-f[TAB]
% julia --trace-compile=test-f[TAB]
```
(However, some shells, e.g. bash-5.0 on Ubuntu, *have* been configured to autocomplete after `--blahfoo=...[TAB]`.)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2432867930/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

> Guideline 6:
>   Each option and option-argument should be a separate argument, except as noted in [Utility Argument Syntax](https://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/9699919799/basedefs/V1_chap12.html#tag_12_01), item (2).

The convention of optionally also allowing the `--arg=param` form of an option argument, in addition to the standard form `--arg param` comes from the GNU glibc function [getopt_long()](https://linux.die.net/man/3/getopt_long) AFAIK, so that's what users are likely have come to expect from lots and lots of other existing command-line utilities using that popular command-line parser function.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2432921908/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/1083","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/1083/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/1083/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/1083/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/juliaup/issues/1083","id":2613763621,"node_id":"I_kwDOEE6KR86byt4l","number":1083,"title":"add a "mature" channel aimed at new student users","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-10-25T10:51:39Z","updated_at":"2024-10-25T10:51:39Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"As an instructor teaching a university course involving Julia-based practicals, I'm not sure which of the existing `juliaup` channels I should recommend to my students to use:
* `release` may at times catapult them onto a *very* fresh release (patch level 1.*x*.0, just a few days old, where the ecosystem has not yet had time to discover and iron out new incompatibilities and glitches).
*  `lts` may at times lead to a very old release, lagging years behind the state of the art.

Students in courses are usually first-time Julia users without any experience of making such judgement calls themselves. They deserve the smoothest possible experience as new Julia users, and neither `release` nor `lts` are in practice ideal for that.

I would like to see a `mature` channel that tracks `release` with a cautious/conservative delay, such that it roughly

* updates to a new “minor” release version only once per year, during the summer break (late July / early August), well before the start of the next [academic year](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Academic_year) in the northern hemisphere
* avoids updating to new “minor” 1.*x* releases until issues have calmed down and there hasn't been a significant new patch release for at least ~5 weeks
* delays updating to new patch releases for ~2–3 weeks, to leave some time for monitoring of new issues introduced by those

In case the current `lts` release is reasonably recent, `mature` might also linger on that until the end of the academic year.

(There might of course also be demand for a `mature-south` channel more in sync with the academic years of schools in the southern hemisphere.)

Managing such a `mature` channel is of course a bit more involved than the other channels, as it involves making judgement calls and liaising with various release managers and triaging teams in the Julia community, to get a sense of how “mature” a minor or a patch release has become.

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3953","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3953/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3953/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3953/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3953","id":2411668181,"node_id":"I_kwDOBOhtSc6PvyLV","number":3953,"title":"Document two new ways of specifying test-specific dependencies","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-07-16T17:17:17Z","updated_at":"2024-07-16T17:17:17Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The [**Test-specific dependencies**](https://pkgdocs.julialang.org/dev/creating-packages/#Test-specific-dependencies) section of the documentation currently only lists two older methods for specifying test-specific dependencies:
* `[targets]` + `[extras]` in main `Project.toml`
* `test/Project.toml`: add *only additional* dependencies there

Both of these older methods have been somewhat unsatisfactory: they only work via special magic applied by `Pkg.test()`, and they do not work if one simply tries to activate a local test environment with `julia --project=test/ runtests.jl`.

Fortunately there are now two new, alternative, better ways of solving this problem, which do not rely on special magic applied by `Pkg.test()`, and these finally allow the test folder to be just a normal project:

* [`[source]`](https://pkgdocs.julialang.org/dev/toml-files/#The-[sources]-section) in `test/Project.toml`: under `[deps]` add `ProjectName=UUID` as a dependency and under `[source]` also add `ProjectName={path='..'}` such that the `cd test/ && julia --project` environment finds the project to test in its own parent folder
* [`[workspace]`](https://pkgdocs.julialang.org/dev/toml-files/#The-[workspace]-section) in `Project.toml`: add `projects = ["test"]` such that the test environment finds the project to test in its own parent folder

At the moment, these new features are still hidden in the [`Project.toml` format description](https://pkgdocs.julialang.org/dev/toml-files/#Project.toml)

It would be great to have these documented as well under https://pkgdocs.julialang.org/dev/creating-packages/#Test-specific-dependencies

manual","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":115619,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMTU2MTk=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/doc","name":"doc","color":"02d7e1","default":false,"description":"This change adds or pertains to documentation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-02-29T21:22:50Z","updated_at":"2024-02-29T21:22:51Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I'm currently re-reading the [Multi-processing and Distributed Computing](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/distributed-computing/#Multi-processing-and-Distributed-Computing) section of the manual, and though I'll note down here some oddities that I think need editorial attention. Work in progress ...

### The last paragraph of the introduction is very odd:
>[@async](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/parallel/#Base.@async) is similar to [@spawnat](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/stdlib/Distributed/#Distributed.@spawnat), but only runs tasks on the local process. We use it to create a "feeder" task for each process. Each task picks the next index that needs to be computed, then waits for its process to finish, then repeats until we run out of indices. Note that the feeder tasks do not begin to execute until the main task reaches the end of the [@sync](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/parallel/#Base.@sync) block, at which point it surrenders control and waits for all the local tasks to complete before returning from the function. As for v0.7 and beyond, the feeder tasks are able to share state via nextidx because they all run on the same process. Even if Tasks are scheduled cooperatively, locking may still be required in some contexts, as in [asynchronous I/O](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/faq/#faq-async-io). This means context switches only occur at well-defined points: in this case, when [remotecall_fetch](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/stdlib/Distributed/#Distributed.remotecall_fetch-Tuple{Any,%20Integer,%20Vararg{Any}}) is called. This is the current state of implementation and it may change for future Julia versions, as it is intended to make it possible to run up to N Tasks on M Process, aka [M:N Threading](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Thread_(computing)#Models). Then a lock acquiring\releasing model for nextidx will be needed, as it is not safe to let multiple processes read-write a resource at the same time.

Everything before makes complete sense and is easy to understand and follow. Then comes this paragraph that left me rather baffled. Who is "We"? The reader and author? The implementors of Distributed? What is all this about feeder tasks? Is this a sudden dive into an implementation detail, completely irrelevant to the beginner-level reader at this point of their journey into Distributed? Why a reference to "As for 0.7", a historic version number likely unfamiliar to a younger Julia audience. What is the reference to nextidx about? It sounds like some example code, but there isn't any in this section that uses that variable. This paragraph is not a shining corner of the manual. Would anything be lost by deleting it?

### Minor edits
* "tensor-rank reducing" -> "array dimensionality reducing" (no need at all here to refer to tensors, an advanced mathematical concept the vast majority of readers will be unfamiliar with)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53537/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/53537/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

match() easier to call again","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":269951426,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwyNjk5NTE0MjY=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/strings","name":"strings","color":"006b75","default":false,"description":""Strings!""},{"id":909268321,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw5MDkyNjgzMjE=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/feature","name":"feature","color":"5664ff","default":false,"description":"Indicates new feature / enhancement requests"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2023-09-22T21:00:35Z","updated_at":"2023-10-02T07:42:42Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I would like to propose the following minor extension method for [match()](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/strings/#Base.match):

>     match(r::Regex, s::Union{SubString{String}, String},
>           idx::Ref{Int}[, addopts])
> 
> If `idx` is a reference (of type `Ref{Int}`), then the dereferenced value `idx[]` is used as the start index for the search. If a match is found, then `idx[]` is updated to the index of the first character after that match. If the match extends to the end of `s`, then `idx[] == firstindex(str) + ncodeunits(str)`.
> 
> # Example
> ```julia
> julia> s = "10+2"; i = Ref{Int}(firstindex(s));
> 
> julia> match(r"\G\d+", s, i)
> RegexMatch("10")
> 
> julia> match(r"\G\d+", s, i)
> 
> julia> match(r"\G\+\d+", s, i)
> RegexMatch("+2")
> 
> julia> match(r"\G\+\d+", s, i)
> 
> julia> i[]
> 5
> 

Proposed implementation:

```
function match(re::Regex, str::Union{SubString{String}, String},
               idx::Ref{Int}, add_opts::UInt32=UInt32(0))
    m = match(re, str, idx[], add_opts)
    if m !== nothing
        idx[] = m.offset + ncodeunits(m.match)
    end
    return m
end
```

This little extension makes `match` much easier to use as a lex-like scanner when writing parsers. The text to be parsed is loaded into a string, and the parser can now use match to scan the next token, using a regular expression starting with `\G` to anchor the match at the location of the index. In order to be able to move the regex anchor `\G` through the string to be parsed, from token to token, it is currently necessary for the caller to update `idx` after each match found to the new value `idx = m.offset + m.ncodeunits(m.match)`, which can then be passed on to the next call of `match()`.

Such parser code becomes much less cluttered if the `match` function can perform that update of `idx` itself.

The proposed functionality has long been available in Perl, in form of the regex matching modifier options `m/\G.../gc`. {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51429","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51429/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51429/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51429/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51429","id":1909514379,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5x0OCL","number":51429,"title":"make match() easier to call again","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-09-22T21:00:35Z","updated_at":"2023-09-22T21:00:35Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"I would like to propose the following minor extension method for [match()](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/strings/#Base.match):

>     match(r::Regex, s::Union{SubString{String}, String},
>           idx::Ref{Int}[, addopts])
> 
> If `idx` is a reference (of type `Ref{Int}`), then the dereferenced value `idx[]` is used as the start index for the search. If a match is found, then `idx[]` is updated to the index of the first character after that match. If the match extends to the end of `s`, then `idx[] == firstindex(str) + ncodeunits(str)`.
> 
> # Example
> ```julia
> julia> s = "10+2"; i = Ref{Int}(firstindex(s));
> 
> julia> match(r"\G\d+", s, i)
> RegexMatch("10")
> 
> julia> match(r"\G\d+", s, i)
> 
> julia> match(r"\G\+\d+", s, i)
> RegexMatch("+2")
> 
> julia> match(r"\G\+\d+", s, i)
> 
> julia> i[]
> 5
> 

Proposed implementation:

```
function match(re::Regex, str::Union{SubString{String}, String},
               idx::Ref{Int}, add_opts::UInt32=UInt32(0))
    m = match(re, str, idx[], add_opts)
    if m !== nothing
        idx[] = m.offset + ncodeunits(m.match)
    end
    return m
end
```

This little extension makes `match` much easier to use as a lex-like scanner when writing parsers. The text to be parsed is loaded into a string, and the parser can now use match to scan the next token, using a regular expression starting with `\G` to anchor the match at the location of the index. In order to be able to move the regex anchor `\G` through the string to be parsed, from token to token, it is currently necessary for the caller to update `idx` after each match found to the new value `idx = m.offset + m.ncodeunits(m.match)`, which can then be passed on to the next call of `match()`.

Such parser code becomes much less cluttered if the `match` function can perform that update of `idx` itself.

The proposed functionality has long been available in Perl, in form of the regex matching modifier options `m/\G.../gc`. See the section `"\G assertion"` in `man perlop` for detailed application examples. Handing over `idx` as a reference to be updated would essentially become Julia's equivalent of that.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51429/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/51429/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

PR implements and documents a cohesive, extensible rounding interface. The code that implements it is in base/rounding.jl

```julia
trunc(x; kwargs...) = round(x, RoundToZero; kwargs...)
floor(x; kwargs...) = round(x, RoundDown; kwargs...)
 ceil(x; kwargs...) = round(x, RoundUp; kwargs...)
round(x; kwargs...) = round(x, RoundNearest; kwargs...)

trunc(::Type{T}, x; kwargs...) where {T} = round(T, x, RoundToZero; kwargs...)
floor(::Type{T}, x; kwargs...) where {T} = round(T, x, RoundDown; kwargs...)
 ceil(::Type{T}, x; kwargs...) where {T} = round(T, x, RoundUp; kwargs...)
round(::Type{T}, x; kwargs...) where {T} = round(T, x, RoundNearest; kwargs...)

round(::Type{T}, x, r::RoundingMode) where {T} = convert(T, round(x, r))
```

And an explanation of how to use it is in docs/src/manual/interfaces.md.

Fixes #50778
Fixes #42060, though maybe this is a bad thing.
Closes #47128

This PR also refactors some implementations of rounding for concrete types to use this new interface more naturally.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50812/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50812/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1694678792","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/50812#issuecomment-1694678792","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/50812","id":1694678792,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5lAr8I","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-08-27T14:10:40Z","updated_at":"2023-08-27T14:10:40Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Could we also have a mode that doesn't throw `InexactError`, and always simply gives us the nearest element of the output type, as in

The problem here is that this causes duplicate overflow/clamp test branches: one in clamp and a redundant one in convert. Are we confident the compiler is able to reliably eliminate these in all cases?

[P.S.: another problem of this kind of construct, where rounding and converting are separate operations, is that it doesn't provide control over the rounding method used when converting e.g. a `UInt32` into a `Float32`  (recall that `Float32` can't represent most integers over `2^24` due to its 24-bit mantissa, i.e. rounding has to happen to the nearest valid mantissa value, not the nearest integer, and therefore the rounding algorithm needs to know the destination type.]

I still believe it makes a lot of sense to treat rounding, clamping and converting as one single basic operation, both in terms of performance, flexibility and accuracy. (Treatment of `NaN` during conversion seems an entirely independent issue.)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1699297594/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

julia> stat("/nonexistantfile")
StatStruct for "/nonexistantfile"

doesn't look like a robust error-handling mechanism either.

I associate them with natural numbers, but their idiosyncratic representation makes them unintuitive to interpret, so I end-up opting for `Integers` just for the sake of decimal representation even when only `Unsigned` would suffice.
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/30167/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/30167/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1655486602","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/30167#issuecomment-1655486602","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/30167","id":1655486602,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5irLiK","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-07-28T10:54:02Z","updated_at":"2023-07-28T10:54:02Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"MATLAB has a quite useful [`format` command](https://uk.mathworks.com/help/matlab/ref/format.html) to change the default output display style of numbers when values are printed by its REPL. It permits control over a choice of different floating-point number formats (short, long, E, G, Eng), as well as integers (decimal, hex), and the amount of white space (compact, loose) used when values are output.

Julia has already an [IOContext](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/io-network/#Base.IOContext) type that similarly controls some pretty-printing parameters (e.g., compact, limit, displaysize, typeinfo, color).

My suggestions would be to extend `Base.IOContext` to also provide control over number formatting, similar to what MATLAB's [`format`](https://uk.mathworks.com/help/matlab/ref/format.html) offers, including
* decimal/hex preference for integers
* precision and exponent-notation preferences for floats.

Note that this would *not* be a change to the Julia language or syntax, or even to the default behaviour of the REPL, but it would just be an extension of capabilities to `Base.IOContext` and some of the `show` methods involved.

Users annoyed by hexadecimal `UInt8[0xff, 0xff]` displays could then simply change their dec/hex preference via an `IOContext`, including the REPL default one in [`Base.active_repl.options.iocontext` dictionary](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/stdlib/REPL/#The-Julian-mode), and instead get `UInt8[255, 255]` shown.

I suspect that may be a very useful extension that might address large parts of the `UInt` concerns raised in this issue (and also give users more control over the float array notation).

Some things could be done better than MATLAB, e.g. instead of just short/long float notation, we could allow more fine-grained control over the number of significant digits shown (lossy output).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1655486602/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

consulting on the slack channel, I am still not aware of a good reason why `Unsigned` types are printed in the hexadecimal base (looks like it was a decision that inherited from C). But there is a number of reasons to represent them in the usual decimal base. I associate them with natural numbers, but their idiosyncratic representation makes them unintuitive to interpret, so I end-up opting for `Integers` just for the sake of decimal representation even when only `Unsigned` would suffice.

```
Base.active_repl.options.iocontext[:uint_base] = 8;
Base.active_repl.options.iocontext[:uint_digits] = 0;
```
to switch from
```
julia> show(UInt8[0,8,64,255])
UInt8[0x00, 0x08, 0x40, 0xff]
```
to
```
julia> show(UInt8[0,8,64,255])
UInt8[0o0, 0x10, 0o100, 0o377]
```
i.e. in octal without any leading digits, as unsigned integers where *really* meant to be appreciated.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1655526425/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49674","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49674/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49674/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49674/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49674","id":1699045466,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5lRWBa","number":49674,"title":"Make `Base.TTY` stable API","user":{"login":"ranocha","id":12693098,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEyNjkzMDk4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/12693098?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ranocha","html_url":"https://github.com/ranocha","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ranocha/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ranocha/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ranocha/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ranocha/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ranocha/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ranocha/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ranocha/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ranocha/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ranocha/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-05-07T12:33:54Z","updated_at":"2023-05-10T15:40:38Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The new precompilation and latency reduction features are really great - thanks a lot to everyone involved. However, there is a slight problem that makes me feel a bit uncomfortable when trying to take advantage of it - precompiling `show` methods and other methods printing to `stdout` (by default). The issue is that `stdout` and `stderr` are not the same at precompile time as at standard execution time. Specifically, I get `Base.TTY` as `typeof(stdout)` in a regular Julia session (on Linux) while it something else during precompilation. Thus, we cannot use `typeof(stdout)` in precompilation code but must use `Base.TTY` explicitly (see https://github.com/trixi-framework/Trixi.jl/pull/1428). @timholy recommended this on Slack and it should be fine since this behavior has been stable since before Julia v1.0. However, `Base.TTY` is not documented (it does not have a docstring) and is not mentioned in the docstring of `stdout` etc. Thus, it is an internal implementation detail that may change any time (strictly speaking). 

It would be great if `Base.TTY` could be documented as stable API for `stdout` during regular execution (without `redirect_stdout` etc.) to clarify that this usage is supported. Alternatively, it would be great to have a stable API alternative to enable precompilation for `stdout` with the same type it has at regular execution time.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49674/reactions","total_count":3,"+1":3,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49674/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1542425376","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49674#issuecomment-1542425376","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/49674","id":1542425376,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5b74sg","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-05-10T15:40:38Z","updated_at":"2023-05-10T15:40:38Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Also:
```
$ echo 'println(typeof(stdin))' >test.jl
$ julia test.jl
Base.TTY
$ julia <test.jl
IOStream
$ cat test.jl | julia
Base.PipeEndpoint
```

Is it actually useful in practice to dynamically dispatch based on what is at the other end of `stdin` and `stdout`?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1542425376/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

    a[i] = read(s, T)
end
```

 """
-    read!(stream::IO, array::AbstractArray)
-    read!(filename::AbstractString, array::AbstractArray)
+    read!(stream::IO, a::AbstractArray{T})
+    read!(stream::IO, a::Ref{T})
+    read!(filename::AbstractString, a::Union{AbstractArray{T}, Ref{T}})
+
+Read `length(a)` consecutive values of type `T` from an I/O stream or
+file into `a`, returning `a`.
+
+If `a` is an `Array` and `isbitstype(T)` is true, this function reads
+`size(a)` bytes directly into memory. Otherwise, each element of
+`a::AbstractArray{T}` is read with a call to `read(stream, T)`.
+
+The `Ref{T}` variant is not intended for use if `isbitstype(T)` is

I have an issue when saving figures with matplotlib on Julia 1.8.2 : the PC shuts down with a message saying that an error has occured and the computer needs a restart.
I noticed the problem was solved when I modified the filename of the output figure. I thought maybe the problem was the length of the filename: maybe the maximum threshold of accepted bytes was exceeded, which made the computer crash (although I find this reaction a little extreme...)
However, when I have a look at the result of `varinfo()`, I notice that the computer crashes when the filename is 159 bytes but it does not crash when the filename is 165 bytes...
So I am wondering what is happening here...

@giordano pointed out that the `exec` command looks like it might have too many quotes. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46698","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46698/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46698/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46698/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46698","id":1368570314,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5RkrnK","number":46698,"title":"Upgrade julia version from the REPL","user":{"login":"curio-sitas","id":26415466,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjI2NDE1NDY2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/26415466?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/curio-sitas","html_url":"https://github.com/curio-sitas","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/curio-sitas/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/curio-sitas/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/curio-sitas/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/curio-sitas/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/curio-sitas/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/curio-sitas/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/curio-sitas/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/curio-sitas/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/curio-sitas/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2022-09-10T09:52:05Z","updated_at":"2022-09-11T20:49:46Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"It would be nice and clean to have a way to upgrade julia to a new version (or even switch between them) directly from the REPL.
The package environment may be transfered and updated at the same time.

One may implement this as a julia command or a `Pkg.jl` REPL command.

For example one could implement a command :
```
] switch 1.7.3
```

- trying to detect how the currently running julia binary was installed and deciding if the current user has permissions to rerun that installation procedure
- running a little shell command line as a detached background process that tries to start that installation procedure after having waited for the current julia process to terminate
- terminating the current julia process (or even all julia processes running the same binary), especially on Windows, where mandatory file locking does not permit applications to run while they are being replaced

The practical problem is that there are many different ways of installing Julia, and these are quite platform dependent.

It's doable, in some circumstances, on some platforms, with lots of restrictions, but I'm not convinced it is worth the effort.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1243040238/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34248","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34248/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34248/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34248/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34248","id":545015303,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU1NDUwMTUzMDM=","number":34248,"title":"A macro that forbids allocation – turning allocation into error","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2020-01-03T14:28:02Z","updated_at":"2022-08-01T10:11:49Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"When implementing real-time applications (robotics control, streaming audio-signal processing, software-defined radio, etc.) in Julia, the unpredictable timing of heap allocation and garbage collection is a significant hazard. Developers who want to use Julia for such applications have to ensure that all required heap memory allocations happen in advance, and not inside time-critical loops. What would help enormously with learning and practicing such allocation-free time-critical programming is a macro that turns allocations into errors. The main purposes of such a macro would be:
* Developers can get compile-time feedback on whether they have succeeded in implementing algorithms in a way that cannot trigger allocations and garbage collection
* Users of existing allocation-free methods can see in the source code a compiler-enforced promise that this method will not cause allocations

This feature suggestion was originally made by @rdeits at the end of his wonderful JuliaCon 2018 talk “JuliaRobotics: Making robots walk with Julia”
https://youtu.be/dmWQtI3DFFo?t=2263
(also mentioning @tkoolen ) in response to my question of what tools he could envisage to make allocation-free real-time programming easier in Julia:
https://youtu.be/dmWQtI3DFFo?t=2160
@JeffBezanson then asked “Would it be enough for the turn-allocation-into-error feature to be lexically scoped”, and after the speaker agreed, promised “OK, you got it!” (followed by audience applause):
https://youtu.be/dmWQtI3DFFo?t=2330

What is the status of this feature?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34248/reactions","total_count":13,"+1":13,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34248/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1200998482","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34248#issuecomment-1200998482","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/34248","id":1200998482,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5HlchS","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-08-01T10:11:49Z","updated_at":"2022-08-01T10:11:49Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Related news: there is a sampling allocation profiler coming in Julia 1.8:

to form Unicode hex ranges in a regular expression causes a `LoadError`:
```julia
julia> r"[\x{00A0}-\x{10FFFD}]"
```
yields
```
ERROR: LoadError: PCRE compilation error: range out of order in character class at offset 11
Stacktrace:
 [1] error(s::String)
   @ Base ./error.jl:33
 [2] compile(pattern::String, options::UInt32)
   @ Base.PCRE ./pcre.jl:155
 [3] compile(regex::Regex)
   @ Base ./regex.jl:82
 [4] Regex(pattern::String, compile_options::UInt32, match_options::UInt32)
   @ Base ./regex.jl:47
 [5] Regex(pattern::String)
   @ Base ./regex.jl:70
 [6] var"@r_str"(__source__::LineNumberNode, __module__::Module, pattern::Any, flags::Vararg{Any})
   @ Base ./regex.jl:119
in expression starting at REPL[45]:1
```

The result should be a regex that matches all Unicode codepoints from `U+00A0` to `U+10FFFD`.
Julia version: 1.7.3","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46137/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46137/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"reopened"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1203822797","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46137#issuecomment-1203822797","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46137","id":1203822797,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5HwODN","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-08-03T11:26:25Z","updated_at":"2022-08-03T11:26:25Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"The command

```
julia> r"[\x{00A0}-\x{10FFFD}]"
```

is short for

```
julia> using Base.PCRE
julia> Regex(raw"[\x{00A0}-\x{10FFFD}]",
             PCRE.UTF | PCRE.MATCH_INVALID_UTF | PCRE.ALT_BSUX | PCRE.UCP,
             PCRE.NO_UTF_CHECK)
ERROR: PCRE compilation error: range out of order in character class at offset 11
Stacktrace:
 [1] error(s::String)
   @ Base ./error.jl:33
 [2] compile(pattern::String, options::UInt32)
   @ Base.PCRE ./pcre.jl:155
 [3] compile(regex::Regex)
   @ Base ./regex.jl:82
 [4] Regex(pattern::String, compile_options::UInt32, match_options::UInt32)
   @ Base ./regex.jl:47
 [5] top-level scope
   @ REPL[4]:1
```

In the latter form, we can play with the compile and match option flags that are passed to the PCRE2 library to specify what flavour of regular-expression behaviour exactly we want.

Doing that, I quickly found that dropping the `PCRE.ALT_BSUX` compile option suppresses this compilation error:

```
julia> Regex(raw"[\x{00A0}-\x{10FFFD}]",
                    PCRE.UTF | PCRE.MATCH_INVALID_UTF | PCRE.UCP,
                    PCRE.NO_UTF_CHECK)
Regex("[\\x{00A0}-\\x{10FFFD}]",0x040a0000)
```

Now it is time to actually read the PCRE2 documentation:
```
man pcre2
man pcre2api
```
There we find indeed the answer:
```
         PCRE2_ALT_BSUX

       This  option  request  alternative  handling of three escape sequences,
       which makes PCRE2's behaviour more like  ECMAscript  (aka  JavaScript).
       When it is set:

       (1) \U matches an upper case "U" character; by default \U causes a com‐
       pile time error (Perl uses \U to upper case subsequent characters).

       (2) \u matches a lower case "u" character unless it is followed by four
       hexadecimal  digits,  in  which case the hexadecimal number defines the
       code point to match. By default, \u causes a compile time  error  (Perl
       uses it to upper case the following character).

       (3)  \x matches a lower case "x" character unless it is followed by two
       hexadecimal digits, in which case the hexadecimal  number  defines  the
       code  point  to  match. By default, as in Perl, a hexadecimal number is
       always expected after \x, but it may have zero, one, or two digits (so,
       for example, \xz matches a binary zero character followed by z).

       ECMAscript 6 added additional functionality to \u. This can be accessed
       using the PCRE2_EXTRA_ALT_BSUX extra option  (see  "Extra  compile  op‐
       tions" below).  Note that this alternative escape handling applies only
       to patterns. Neither of these options affects  the  processing  of  re‐
       placement strings passed to pcre2_substitute().
```

In other words, Julia asks PCRE2 to implement a slightly more JavaScript-compatible version of regular expressions than the more Perl-compatible flavor it would have given us by default. The man page doesn't explicitly say so, but they way I read it, `\x{xxxx}` seems not part of the ECMAscript syntax, and is in fact therefore identical to just `x{xxxx}`. So in other words, you get the same error with
```
julia> r"[x{00A0}-x{10FFFD}]"
ERROR: LoadError: PCRE compilation error: range out of order in character class at offset 9
```
And it suddenly all makes sense, because `}-x` is indeed an out-of-range order.

I guess that choice in favour of ECMAscript syntax for `\u`, `\U` and `\x` warrants to be examined, justified, and documented. (Ideally, I think the Julia manual should contain a self-contained reference of the regular-expression syntax supported.)

So this is clearly not a bug in the PCRE2 C library, but at least an omission in the Julia manual.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1203822797/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

*  afa14048a5551fec9ee79f9472a16fb10e260b56 in Jan 2015 replaced PCRE compile option  `PCRE.JAVASCRIPT_COMPAT` with PCRE2 option `PCRE.ALT_BSUX` while upgrading from PCRE to PCRE2, i.e. this seems to be just adjusting to the new API
* 7909e3de17f682d934c1d418a98111e357515400 in Mar 2013 added `PCRE.JAVASCRIPT_COMPAT` to “fix r"\u2220" bug mentioned in #107”

The latter commit was made by @nolta as a “band-air”.

String literals, macro/raw string literals and the resulting differences in quote and backslash escaping clearly had a rather tortuous history in the evolution of Julia. Note that at no point in issue #107 is there any discussion about whether Julia's flavour of PCRE should be more like Perl or more like JavaScript. The choice of the JavaScript variant just happened to cause one error message in one example to disappear, if I understood that discussion correctly.

They wanted `match(r"\u2200", "\u2200")` to match, whereas in Perl-compatible regular-expression syntax it would have had to be `match(r"\x{2200}", "\u2200")` because in Perl RE, `\u` mean “lowercase the next letter”. It appears that you accidentally ran whatever program first created the folder `/Users/schwob/.config` as the superuser `root`, and not as your normal user `schwob`. Since then no other program running as user `schwob` had any ability to create a file in there, or even test for the presence of files in there because that folder ended up not belonging to you.

Solution: change the ownership of that folder and its contents, e.g. with
```
$ sudo chown -R schwob /Users/schwob/.config
```

This is not a Julia issue. Julia should normally never be run as `root`. Julia may not even have been the first program trying to access `/Users/schwob/.config`. This is just a problem in your particular home directory on that machine.

Generally, all files and folders under `/Users/schwob` should be owned by user `schob`. You may want to check if there are other things in there besides `/Users/schwob/.config` that you don't actually own, e.g. with
```
$ find ~ -user root -print
```
and then `chown` them as well.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1206668065/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

                in1, in2, in3, in4, in5, in6)
```

stage)"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2022-08-04T10:45:39Z","updated_at":"2022-08-05T17:34:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Assignment in tuples and arrays

```
julia> x=65; [x,x|=0x20]
2-element Vector{Int64}:
 97
 97

julia> x=65; (x,x|=0x20)
(97, 97)
```

was made a syntax error at one point (in 0.3; see #4982, #4990 and closing commits), but it's allowed again (not sure if intentionally or not). But it leads to some fun inconsistencies:

```
julia> value = 1; (value, value + 1, value += 1, value += 1)
(3, 2, 2, 3)

julia> let value = 1; (value, value + 1, value += 1, value += 1); end
(1, 2, 2, 3)

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46267","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46267/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46267/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46267/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46267","id":1330641183,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5PT_kf","number":46267,"title":"Add a way to set REPL output format to engineering notation","user":{"login":"tgross35","id":13724985,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEzNzI0OTg1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/13724985?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/tgross35","html_url":"https://github.com/tgross35","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tgross35/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tgross35/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tgross35/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tgross35/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tgross35/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tgross35/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tgross35/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tgross35/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/tgross35/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-08-06T04:48:47Z","updated_at":"2022-08-08T17:28:47Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Hello all,

I would like to suggest adding a simple way to configure the output format to use engineering notation - that is, scientific notation that is restricted to powers of three. This sort of formatting is very useful for easy conversion to SI units.

For example, after enabling this feature, something like the following would be expected:

```julia
julia> 1200
1.2e3

julia> 10000
10e3

julia> 0.0001
100e-6

julia> [10 1000 100000 10000000]
1×4 Matrix{Int64}:
 10  1e3  100e3  10e6
```

With this formatting, the outputs are quick to read as 1.2k, 10M, 100µ, etc.

The package `NumericIO` ([link here](https://juliahub.com/ui/Packages/NumericIO/WRjvO/0.3.2?page=0)) implements this functionality via functions, and [this StackOverflow question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/54000507/using-julia-how-to-format-avogadros-constant-or-other-numbers-in-engineering) includes a solution. However, both options come with overhead (a package or a lengthy function), and requires putting the output through a separate function, rather than native display (though this could likely be changed by overriding `Base.show`, which is another step).

Matlab/Octave allows this with `format short eng` ([link to the relevant MatLab docs here](https://www.mathworks.com/help/matlab/ref/format.html)). 

```octave
octave:1> format compact
octave:2> format short eng
octave:3> 1200
ans = 1.2000e+003
octave:4> 10000
ans = 10.0000e+003
octave:5> 0.0001
ans = 100.0000e-006
octave:6> [10 1000 100000 10000000]
ans =
    10.0000e+00     1.0000e+03   100.0000e+03    10.0000e+06
```

A bonus feature would be some way to do something like `format compact` to not add new lines between interpreter entries (though there may be a way to do this that I just haven't found).

A bonus bonus feature would be a way to enable SI prefixes natively, like in `NumericIO`. So typing e.g. `1.5k` expands to `1500.0`, or `2e-8` displays as `20n`. (I realize that this is likely out of the scope of what the REPL intends to do, but it is an extremely helpful feature when working with science/engineering documentation)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46267/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46267/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1208404628","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46267#issuecomment-1208404628","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46267","id":1208404628,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5IBsqU","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-08-08T17:28:47Z","updated_at":"2022-08-08T17:28:47Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Julia Base has already the dictionary type [IOContext](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/io-network/#Base.IOContext) to configure how functions like [show](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/io-network/#Text-I/O) display values. And you can already use [`Base.active_repl.options.iocontext`](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/stdlib/REPL/#The-Julian-mode) to configure the IOContext that the REPL uses to show the return value of functions.

So I guess what is missing is in IOContext support for a bit more control over the format used for floating-point numbers.

At the moment there is already:

```
julia> Base.active_repl.options.iocontext[:compact] = true; pi*1e4
31415.9

julia> Base.active_repl.options.iocontext[:compact] = false; pi*1e4
31415.926535897932
```

But that single Boolean parameter obviously offers fewer options than MATLAB's [format](https://www.mathworks.com/help/matlab/ref/format.html) command.

In the form of an extended [IOContext docstring](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/io-network/#Base.IOContext), what would a nice suitable interface for the desired functionality look like?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1208404628/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

documentation"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-08-08T14:46:56Z","updated_at":"2022-08-08T18:00:46Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/742b9abb4dd4621b667ec5bb3434b8b3602f96fd/base/docs/basedocs.jl#L1657

The type assertion on the second argument seems to indicate that the type `T` of the input argument should be passed, but in reality it's `Tuple{T}`. E.g:
```julia
julia> f(x::Int) = 2
f (generic function with 1 method)

julia> invoke(f, Int, 2)
ERROR: TypeError: in invoke, expected Type{T} where T<:Tuple, got Type{Int64}
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ REPL[9]:1

julia> invoke(f, Tuple{Int}, 2)
2
```
This ambiguity is of course only for the 1-argument case, and the examples make the usage clear. However, maybe the assertion should be changed to reflect this as well.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46285/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46285/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1208437201","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46285#issuecomment-1208437201","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46285","id":1208437201,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5IB0nR","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-08-08T18:00:46Z","updated_at":"2022-08-08T18:00:46Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"How about the [variadic tuple type](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/devdocs/types/#Tuple-types)
