{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312","id":1335541070,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5Pmr1O","number":46312,"title":"Feature request: Filter argument to `walkdir`","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-08-11T06:47:54Z","updated_at":"2022-08-11T15:06:16Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"When traversing a large directory with sub-directories, there is currently no way of preventing the `walkdir` iterator recursing into an unwanted directory.
This comes up often:
* Perhaps the directory is irrelevant, but 99% of the subdirectories reside there, so most the time is wasted traversing here
* Perhaps a particular directory is mounted to an unresponsive drive/network, and so would stall the iterator for minutes if encountered
* Perhaps the user does not have access to an irrelevant sub-directory and so it would throw an error, and the user does not want to change the `onerror` keyword, because the user actually _does_ want to throw an error if the desired sub-directory is not accessible.

We could change it with a `filter` keyword, or alternatively as the first positional argument to allow `do`-syntax. Any subdirectory `d` for which the filter function `f(d) === false` would be skipped and never descended into.

I think it's warranted to special-case filtering for this iterator in particular, by adding it as an argument to the iterator instead of simply filtering the iterators output. Unlike other iterators, filtering an element away _inside the iterator itself_ can seriously reduce the total number of iterations. And this iterator can have particularly annoying side effects if undesired elements are iterated over.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1212119400","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312#issuecomment-1212119400","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312","id":1212119400,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5IP3lo","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-08-11T15:06:16Z","updated_at":"2022-08-11T15:06:16Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"I agree that a directory-pruning filter is an essential missing feature of [walkdir](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/file/#Base.Filesystem.walkdir). (speaking as a regular user of the equivalent Perl module [`File::Find`](https://perldoc.perl.org/File::Find), which has undergone a lot of performance tuning)

However, I wonder if the proposed interface won't hinder performance:

Since `walkdir` does already prepare an array of subdirectories for each directory that it visits, wouldn't it be more efficient if the proposed `filter` function is not called with `filter(path::String)` for each directory visited, but instead is called as `filter(parent_path::String, dirs::Vector{String})` and is then expected to return a filtered version of `dirs::Vector::{String}` that contains only the subdirectories to be recursed into. Often, filters will operate only on the directory name, rather than the entire path. (A typical example would be to suppress “invisible” `.*` files. Therefore, having the directory name already separated out can save a lot of CPU cycles otherwise needed on string operations.

In addition, there could also be another kwarg parameter `chdir::Boolean=false` that can be enabled such that when `filter` is called, the current working directory is already set to `parent_path`, such that if the filter wants to test some property of the file (e.g., access permissions is a common case), it can hand over to the OS just the short relative filename.

Such a chdir option would also be particularly useful in case we add a `do` version of `walkdir`, which instead of returning an iterator calls a function for each file visited, in which case the act of that user-supplied function doing something to the file can be more efficient if the current working directory is the parent directory. Some operating systems handle file-system requests a lot faster if you give them short relative paths relative to the current working directory, an that's why Perl's [`File::Find`](https://perldoc.perl.org/File::Find) chose that kind of interface.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1212119400/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312","id":1335541070,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5Pmr1O","number":46312,"title":"Feature request: Filter argument to `walkdir`","user":{"login":"jakobnissen","id":23193950,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjIzMTkzOTUw","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/23193950?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen","html_url":"https://github.com/jakobnissen","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jakobnissen/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2022-08-11T06:47:54Z","updated_at":"2022-08-11T15:13:36Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"When traversing a large directory with sub-directories, there is currently no way of preventing the `walkdir` iterator recursing into an unwanted directory.
This comes up often:
* Perhaps the directory is irrelevant, but 99% of the subdirectories reside there, so most the time is wasted traversing here
* Perhaps a particular directory is mounted to an unresponsive drive/network, and so would stall the iterator for minutes if encountered
* Perhaps the user does not have access to an irrelevant sub-directory and so it would throw an error, and the user does not want to change the `onerror` keyword, because the user actually _does_ want to throw an error if the desired sub-directory is not accessible.

We could change it with a `filter` keyword, or alternatively as the first positional argument to allow `do`-syntax. Any subdirectory `d` for which the filter function `f(d) === false` would be skipped and never descended into.

I think it's warranted to special-case filtering for this iterator in particular, by adding it as an argument to the iterator instead of simply filtering the iterators output. Unlike other iterators, filtering an element away _inside the iterator itself_ can seriously reduce the total number of iterations. And this iterator can have particularly annoying side effects if undesired elements are iterated over.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1212128641","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312#issuecomment-1212128641","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312","id":1212128641,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5IP52B","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-08-11T15:13:35Z","updated_at":"2022-08-11T15:13:35Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Another important filter criterion is whether a file has already been visited before. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46314","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46314/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46314/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46314/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46314","id":1335884888,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5Pn_xY","number":46314,"title":"Uninformative error message in `mapreduce` with missing second function argument ","user":{"login":"bvdmitri","id":6557701,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY1NTc3MDE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6557701?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bvdmitri","html_url":"https://github.com/bvdmitri","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bvdmitri/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bvdmitri/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bvdmitri/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bvdmitri/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bvdmitri/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bvdmitri/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bvdmitri/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bvdmitri/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bvdmitri/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-08-11T12:23:16Z","updated_at":"2022-08-11T15:50:37Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"<!--
If you have a question please search or post to our Discourse site: https://discourse.julialang.org.
We use the GitHub issue tracker for bug reports and feature requests only.

If you're submitting a bug report, be sure to include as much relevant information as
possible, including a minimal reproducible example and the output of `versioninfo()`.
If you're experiencing a problem with a particular package, open an issue on that
package's repository instead.

Thanks for contributing to the Julia project!
-->
Consider the following MWE: 

```
collection = ones(2)  # Collection is not empty 
mapreduce(+, collection) # Throws collection is empty error
```

```
MethodError: reducing over an empty collection is not allowed; consider supplying `init` to the reducer
```

Furthermore `init = 0.0` keyword argument does not help and `mapreduce(+, collection; init = 0.0)` throws the same error. The real issue here that some forgot a second function argument. 

It should be 
```
mapreduce(prod, +, collection) # works
```

The error message is bad, suggestion to supply `init` is misleading and un-informative in this case.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46314/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46314/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1212173744","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46314#issuecomment-1212173744","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46314","id":1212173744,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5IQE2w","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-08-11T15:50:37Z","updated_at":"2022-08-11T15:50:37Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Looks like a case where an insufficiently tight function signature for `mapreduce` is causing an unhelpful error message:

Perhaps
```
mapreduce(f, op, A::AbstractArrayOrBroadcasted...; kw...) =
    reduce(op, map(f, A...); kw...)
```
in [base/reducedim.jl:359](base/reducedim.jl) should be changed to
```
mapreduce(f::Function, op::Function, A::AbstractArrayOrBroadcasted...; kw...) =
    reduce(op, map(f, A...); kw...)
```
as you can prototype in the REPL with
```
julia> import Base.reduce, Base.AbstractArrayOrBroadcasted
julia> newmapreduce(f::Function, op::Function, A::AbstractArrayOrBroadcasted...; kw...) =
    reduce(op, map(f, A...); kw...)
julia> collection = ones(2);
julia> newmapreduce(+, collection)
ERROR: MethodError: no method matching newmapreduce(::typeof(+), ::Vector{Float64})
Closest candidates are:
  newmapreduce(::Function, ::Function, ::Union{Base.AbstractBroadcasted, AbstractArray}...; kw...) at REPL[2]:1
```
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1212173744/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Stricter type annotations help preventing mistakes, but also limit composability.

It's one of those areas, where Julia's highly composeable type system, and absence of a full-featured trait system (we can't say in the type signature that “f must have the trait of offering a single-argument method” and “op must offer a two-argument method”), interferes with our ability to catch some pretty obvious errors with meaningful error messages.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1212184940/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

And this iterator can have particularly annoying side effects if undesired elements are iterated over.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1212195979","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312#issuecomment-1212195979","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46312","id":1212195979,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5IQKSL","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-08-11T16:10:11Z","updated_at":"2022-08-11T16:10:11Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Don't forget the time the kernel needs to undo the `joinpath` at the other end. and dispatch","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":"Types, subtyping and method dispatch"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2022-08-12T08:24:35Z","updated_at":"2022-08-12T12:29:44Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"```julia
julia> struct A <: Real end

julia> Base.:(==)(::A, ::A) = false

julia> Base.:(<)(::A, ::A) = false

julia> x = A(); y = x;

julia> (x < y) | (x == y)
false

julia> x <= y
ERROR: <= not defined for A
Stacktrace:
 [1] error(::String, ::String, ::Type)
   @ Base ./error.jl:44
 [2] no_op_err(name::String, T::Type)
   @ Base ./promotion.jl:484
 [3] <=(x::A, y::A)
   @ Base ./promotion.jl:501
 [4] top-level scope
   @ REPL[11]:1

help?> <=
search: <=

  <=(x, y)
  ≤(x,y)

  Less-than-or-equals comparison operator. Falls back to (x < y) | (x == y).
[...]

julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.9.0-DEV.1120
Commit 95cbb9f6184 (2022-08-12 00:44 UTC)
Platform Info:
  OS: Linux (x86_64-linux-gnu)
  CPU: 8 × 11th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-1135G7 @ 2.40GHz
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-14.0.5 (ORCJIT, tigerlake)
  Threads: 1 on 8 virtual cores
Environment:
  LD_LIBRARY_PATH = :/usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/gtk-3.0/modules
  JULIA_EDITOR = subl
```
As per the docstring, `<=` should fall back to using `<` and `==`.

```
<=(x, y) = (x < y) | (x == y)
```
in [base/operators.jl](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/e4a3106affaeb06bf5c6d1db646597598d3c0784/base/operators.jl#L392) is less specific than the line
```

```
in [base/promotion.jl](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/e4a3106affaeb06bf5c6d1db646597598d3c0784/base/promotion.jl#L501), so the latter overrides the former.

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45960","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45960/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45960/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45960/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45960","id":1297715689,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM5NWZHp","number":45960,"title":"allow addenv to delete environment variables","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2022-07-07T16:15:37Z","updated_at":"2022-07-07T16:15:37Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The existing [`Base.withenv()`](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/base/#Base.withenv) function allows users to delete environment variables by mapping them to `nothing`.

Example (from https://github.com/JuliaGraphics/Gtk.jl/issues/647):
```
GTK_ORIG_ENV = ["GDK_PIXBUF_MODULE_FILE" => nothing]
withenv(GTK_ORIG_ENV...) do ; run(`emacs`) ; end
```
It would be nice if the same facility also existed in [`Base.addenv`](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/base/base/#Base.addenv), such that the above line could be written in a more thread-save way as
```
run(addenv(`emacs`, GTK_ORIG_ENV...; inherit=true))
```

The code I am trying to use to connect is as follows:
```
using Distributed
keyFile = raw"~/.ssh/juliakey"
addprocs([("docker@192.168.2.220:2222 0.0.0.0:3000", 1)]; exename="/usr/local/julia/bin/julia", dir="/home/docker", sshflags=["-vvv", "-i", keyFile], exeflags=`--threads=auto`)
```

This error shows, that the SSH connection is working perfectly fine, even a julia instance is started on the correct port (checked that with different tools), however it can't connect. Testing it with two different machines results in this error.
In this case it doesn't matter, whether this machine hosts a docker container with julia and ssh or if I try to use it natievly on windows.
I has nothing to do with the firewall, as I disabled it for testing.

The strange thing is however, that it works when I connect to myself on localhost via ssh.

To add to the confusion, I started two docker containers with julia and ssh on my machine and tried to connect between both, with the same result. It seems, that as soon as we cross the border to another machine, it fails to connect.

I added the dockerfile, if anyone wants to test it.
Steps to reproduce:

1. generate key or use existing key (update compose file) 
2. `docker-compose up`
3. copy key to one of the containers `docker cp .\juliakey dockerjl-ssh2-1:/home/docker/.ssh/juliakey`
4. add IP to known hosts `ssh-keyscan -p 2222 -t rsa 192.168.2.220 >> ~/.ssh/known_hosts`
5. start julia with ` /usr/local/julia/bin/julia`
6. run julia code above in the REPL
7. receive Error message

[DockerJL.zip](https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/files/8660625/DockerJL.zip)

Thanks in advance for any help {"action":"opened","number":46205,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/pulls/46205","id":1011130274,"node_id":"PR_kwDOABkWpM48RJ-i","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46205","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46205.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/46205.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/46205","number":46205,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"How to pass options to julia using #!/usr/bin/env -S","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Option `env -S` for splitting command-line arguments in shebang scripts has been supported on Linux now for a couple of years already, and on FreeBSD and macOS long before that. Therefore we can now recommend using it, and replace the more complicated bash-based method in the FAQ.

[Complicated old answer](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/faq/#How-do-I-pass-options-to-julia-using-#!/usr/bin/env?)

Much simpler new answer:

>### How do I pass options to `julia` using `#!/usr/bin/env`?
>
>Passing options to `julia` in a so-called shebang line, as in `#!/usr/bin/env julia --startup-file=no`, will not work on many platforms (BSD, macOS, Linux) where the kernel, unlike the shell, does not split arguments at space characters. The option `env -S`, which splits a single argument string into multiple arguments at spaces, similar to a shell, offers a simple workaround:
>
>```julia
>#!/usr/bin/env -S julia --color=yes --startup-file=no
>@show ARGS  # put any Julia code here
>```
>
>!!! note
>    Option `env -S` appeared in FreeBSD 6.0 (2005), macOS Sierra (2016) and GNU/Linux coreutils 8.30 (2018).

 ### How do I pass options to `julia` using `#!/usr/bin/env`?
 
-Passing options to `julia` in so-called shebang by, e.g.,
-`#!/usr/bin/env julia --startup-file=no` may not work in some
-platforms such as Linux.  This is because argument parsing in shebang
-is platform-dependent and not well-specified.  In a Unix-like
-environment, a reliable way to pass options to `julia` in an
-executable script would be to start the script as a `bash` script and
-use `exec` to replace the process to `julia`:
+Passing options to `julia` in a so-called shebang line, as in
+`#!/usr/bin/env julia --startup-file=no`, will not work on many
+platforms (BSD, macOS, Linux) where the kernel, unlike the shell, does
+not split arguments at space characters. The option `env -S`, which
+splits a single argument string into multiple arguments at spaces,
+similar to a shell, offers a simple workaround:
 
 ```julia
-#!/bin/bash
-#=
-exec julia --color=yes --startup-file=no "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}" "$@"
-=#
-
+#!/usr/bin/env -S julia --color=yes --startup-file=no
 @show ARGS  # put any Julia code here
 ```
 
-In the example above, the code between `#=` and `=#` is run as a `bash`
-script.  Julia ignores this part since it is a multi-line comment for
-Julia.  The Julia code after `=#` is ignored by `bash` since it stops
-parsing the file once it reaches to the `exec` statement.
-

`env -S` for splitting command-line arguments in shebang scripts has been supported on Linux now for a couple of years already, and on FreeBSD and macOS long before that. Therefore we can now recommend using it, and replace the more complicated bash-based method in the FAQ.

[Complicated old answer](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/faq/#How-do-I-pass-options-to-julia-using-#!/usr/bin/env?)

Much simpler new answer:

>### How do I pass options to `julia` using `#!/usr/bin/env`?
>
>Passing options to `julia` in a so-called shebang line, as in `#!/usr/bin/env julia --startup-file=no`, will not work on many platforms (BSD, macOS, Linux) where the kernel, unlike the shell, does not split arguments at space characters. The option `env -S`, which splits a single argument string into multiple arguments at spaces, similar to a shell, offers a simple workaround:
>
>```julia
>#!/usr/bin/env -S julia --color=yes --startup-file=no
>@show ARGS  # put any Julia code here
>```
>
>!!! note
>    Option `env -S` appeared in FreeBSD 6.0 (2005), macOS Sierra (2016) and GNU/Linux coreutils 8.30 (2018).

-S","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Option `env -S` for splitting command-line arguments in shebang scripts has been supported on Linux now for a couple of years already, and on FreeBSD and macOS long before that. Therefore we can now recommend using it, and replace the more complicated bash-based method in the FAQ.

[Complicated old answer](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/faq/#How-do-I-pass-options-to-julia-using-#!/usr/bin/env?)

Much simpler new answer:

>### How do I pass options to `julia` using `#!/usr/bin/env`?
>
>Passing options to `julia` in a so-called shebang line, as in `#!/usr/bin/env julia --startup-file=no`, will not work on many platforms (BSD, macOS, Linux) where the kernel, unlike the shell, does not split arguments at space characters. The option `env -S`, which splits a single argument string into multiple arguments at spaces, similar to a shell, offers a simple workaround:
>
>```julia
>#!/usr/bin/env -S julia --color=yes --startup-file=no
>@show ARGS  # put any Julia code here
>```
>
>!!! note
>    Option `env -S` appeared in FreeBSD 6.0 (2005), macOS Sierra (2016) and GNU/Linux coreutils 8.30 (2018).

 ### How do I pass options to `julia` using `#!/usr/bin/env`?
 
-Passing options to `julia` in so-called shebang by, e.g.,
-`#!/usr/bin/env julia --startup-file=no` may not work in some
-platforms such as Linux.  This is because argument parsing in shebang
-is platform-dependent and not well-specified.  In a Unix-like
-environment, a reliable way to pass options to `julia` in an
-executable script would be to start the script as a `bash` script and
-use `exec` to replace the process to `julia`:
+Passing options to `julia` in a so-called shebang line, as in
+`#!/usr/bin/env julia --startup-file=no`, will not work on many
+platforms (BSD, macOS, Linux) where the kernel, unlike the shell, does
+not split arguments at space characters. The option `env -S`, which
+splits a single argument string into multiple arguments at spaces,
+similar to a shell, offers a simple workaround:
 
 ```julia
-#!/bin/bash
-#=
-exec julia --color=yes --startup-file=no "${BASH_SOURCE[0]}" "$@"
-=#
-
+#!/usr/bin/env -S julia --color=yes --startup-file=no
 @show ARGS  # put any Julia code here
 ```
 
-In the example above, the code between `#=` and `=#` is run as a `bash`
-script.  Julia ignores this part since it is a multi-line comment for
-Julia.  The Julia code after `=#` is ignored by `bash` since it stops
-parsing the file once it reaches to the `exec` statement.
-

`env -S` for splitting command-line arguments in shebang scripts has been supported on Linux now for a couple of years already, and on FreeBSD and macOS long before that. Therefore we can now recommend using it, and replace the more complicated bash-based method in the FAQ.

[Complicated old answer](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/faq/#How-do-I-pass-options-to-julia-using-#!/usr/bin/env?)

Much simpler new answer:

>### How do I pass options to `julia` using `#!/usr/bin/env`?
>
>Passing options to `julia` in a so-called shebang line, as in `#!/usr/bin/env julia --startup-file=no`, will not work on many platforms (BSD, macOS, Linux) where the kernel, unlike the shell, does not split arguments at space characters. The option `env -S`, which splits a single argument string into multiple arguments at spaces, similar to a shell, offers a simple workaround:
>
>```julia
>#!/usr/bin/env -S julia --color=yes --startup-file=no
>@show ARGS  # put any Julia code here
>```
>
>!!! note
>    Option `env -S` appeared in FreeBSD 6.0 (2005), macOS Sierra (2016) and GNU/Linux coreutils 8.30 (2018).

-S","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Option `env -S` for splitting command-line arguments in shebang scripts has been supported on Linux now for a couple of years already, and on FreeBSD and macOS long before that. Therefore we can now recommend using it, and replace the more complicated bash-based method in the FAQ.

[Complicated old answer](https://docs.julialang.org/en/v1/manual/faq/#How-do-I-pass-options-to-julia-using-#!/usr/bin/env?)

Much simpler new answer:

>### How do I pass options to `julia` using `#!/usr/bin/env`?
>
>Passing options to `julia` in a so-called shebang line, as in `#!/usr/bin/env julia --startup-file=no`, will not work on many platforms (BSD, macOS, Linux) where the kernel, unlike the shell, does not split arguments at space characters. The option `env -S`, which splits a single argument string into multiple arguments at spaces, similar to a shell, offers a simple workaround:
>
>```julia
>#!/usr/bin/env -S julia --color=yes --startup-file=no
>@show ARGS  # put any Julia code here
>```
>
>!!! note
>    Option `env -S` appeared in FreeBSD 6.0 (2005), macOS Sierra (2016) and GNU/Linux coreutils 8.30 (2018).

`mean`","user":{"login":"wsshin","id":1783289,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3ODMyODk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1783289?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/wsshin","html_url":"https://github.com/wsshin","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/wsshin/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/wsshin/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/wsshin/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/wsshin/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/wsshin/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/wsshin/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/wsshin/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/wsshin/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/wsshin/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":171885699,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNzE4ODU2OTk=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/maths","name":"maths","color":"009800","default":false,"description":"Mathematical functions"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2022-05-04T13:52:40Z","updated_at":"2022-05-10T18:00:32Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"This issue tries to initiate a discussion to solve the issue reported in https://github.com/JuliaStats/StatsBase.jl/issues/196.  

In `StatsBase.jl`, we calculate the Z-scores of data by `zscore()`.  The Z-scores are a shift-and-scaled version of data such that they have a zero mean and unit standard deviation.  For example, the Z-scores of a vector `x` are calculated as follows:
```julia
using Statistics

μ = mean(x)
σ = std(x, mean=μ)
z = (x .- μ) ./ σ
```

The `StatsBase.jl` issue linked above reports that the Z-scores are calculated inaccurately when all the entries of `x` are identical.  For `x` with all-identical entries `x0`, in exact arithmetic, `μ` should be `x0` and `σ` should be `0`, so the entries of `z` should be `0/0`, which is `NaN`.  However, in floating-point arithmetic, the Z-scores are not `NaN` due to rounding errors.  An example taken from the original issue:
```julia-repl
julia> using Statistics

julia> x0 = log(1e-5)
-11.512925464970229

julia> x = fill(x0, 8)
8-element Vector{Float64}:
 -11.512925464970229
 -11.512925464970229
 -11.512925464970229
 -11.512925464970229
 -11.512925464970229
 -11.512925464970229
 -11.512925464970229
 -11.512925464970229

julia> μ = mean(x)  # slightly different from x0
-11.512925464970227

julia> σ = std(x)  # not 0 because μ ≠ x0
1.89900533991096e-15

julia> z = (x .- μ) ./ σ  # not NaN because μ ≠ x0 and σ ≠ 0
8-element Vector{Float64}:
 -0.9354143466934853
 -0.9354143466934853
 -0.9354143466934853
 -0.9354143466934853
 -0.9354143466934853
 -0.9354143466934853
 -0.9354143466934853
 -0.9354143466934853
```

The problem can be avoided if `mean` is calculated accurately when `x` has all-identical entries.  The most obvious option is to return `x[1]` if `x` has all-identical entries.  However, checking if `x` has all-identical entries is very slow when `x` is long and has indeed all-identical entries (in which case `all()` below does not short-circuit):
```julia-repl
julia> x = fill(rand(), 10000);

julia> @btime mean($x);
  1.186 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)

julia> @btime all(y->(y==$(x[1])), $x);  # much slower than mean()
  6.311 μs (0 allocations: 0 bytes)
```

Another option proposed in the original issue was to refine the calculated mean.  This option calculates the mean of `x .- μ` as the refinement value `∆μ`:
```julia
function mean_refined(x)
    μ = mean(x)
    ∆μ = mean(y->(y-μ), x)
    μ += ∆μ

    return μ
end
```
This is 2X slower than `mean()` because it calls `mean()` twice internally, but it calculates the mean accurately for `x` with all-identical entries, except for very extreme cases where `length(x)` is in the order of `1e15` for double-precision `x`; see the explanation at https://github.com/JuliaStats/StatsBase.jl/issues/196#issuecomment-1079840873).  A demonstration of the successful refinement of the previous example:
```julia-repl
julia> x0 = log(1e-5)
-11.512925464970229

julia> x = fill(x0, 8);

julia> μ = mean_refined(x)  # μ == x0
-11.512925464970229

julia> σ = std(x, mean=μ)  # σ == 0
0.0

julia> z = (x .- μ) ./ σ  # correctly NaN
8-element Vector{Float64}:
 NaN
 NaN
 NaN
 NaN
 NaN
 NaN
 NaN
 NaN
```
In order to minimize the chance of performance degradation, we could perform `mean_refined()` only when the first few entries of `x` are identical (as `mean_refined()` is meaningful only for `x` with nearly identical entries).  Alternatively, we can introduce a keyword argument `refine::Bool` to `mean()`, such that `mean()` performs `mean_refined()` only when `refine = true`.

What would be the best approach to solve this issue?  I note that an option to implement a more accurate `sum()` inside `mean()` was also suggested in the original issue.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45186/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45186/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1122704179","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45186#issuecomment-1122704179","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/45186","id":1122704179,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM5C6xsz","user":{"login":"mgkuhn","id":4479402,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0Nzk0MDI=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/4479402?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn","html_url":"https://github.com/mgkuhn","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mgkuhn/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-05-10T18:00:32Z","updated_at":"2022-05-10T18:00:32Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"There is also the algorithm
```
function mean(x)
    m = first(x)
    for k = firstindex(x)+1:lastindex(x)
        m += (x[k] - m) / k
    end
    m
end
```
which can be numerically more stable. It uses only a single pass over the array, but is a bit more cumbersome to parallelize efficiently (than a simple `mapreduce` call).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1122704179/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

The way `Distributed` works is:
* `addprocs` makes an SSH connection to worker W
* on worker W julia is started, calls `getipaddr()` and returns that IP address A
* `addprocs` now makes a TCP connection to address A

If A is an address behind a NAT, `ssh A` will obviously fail to reach the same machine as `ssh W`, so make sure there is no NAT between you and the worker. You can't use NAT for protocols that communicate numerical IP addresses, whether that is ftp, SIP or `Distributed`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1122717081/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I'd like a worker that has been invoked via SSH instead to bind to the same IP address as SSH did, because that is known to work, and can usually be fetched from the third word in the environment variable `SSH_CONNECTION`, as in
```
--- a/stdlib/Distributed/src/cluster.jl
+++ b/stdlib/Distributed/src/cluster.jl
@@ -1276,7 +1276,12 @@ function init_bind_addr()
     else
         bind_port = 0
         try
-            bind_addr = string(getipaddr())
+            if haskey(ENV, "SSH_CONNECTION")
+                # reuse the IP address that ssh already used to get here
+                bind_addr = split(ENV["SSH_CONNECTION"], ' ')[3]
+            else
+                bind_addr = string(first(filter(!islinklocaladdr, getipaddrs())))
+            end
         catch
             # All networking is unavailable, initialize bind_addr to the loopback address
             # Will cause an exception to be raised only when used.
```

However that patch alone will likely break things, because SSH might have used an IPv6 address, and `Distributed` can't cope with IPv6 addresses yet.

I wrote a half-finished IPv6 patch for Distributed back in the days of Julia 1.3. I'll dig it out and try to finish it into a PR, and hope that will eventually solve your problem as well. Until then, just make sure that your worker has *exactly one* IPv4 address (except for loopback).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/1129847562/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Unicode contains a huge number of exotic and rarely-used characters, of which the Braille symbols are only a tiny tiny fraction. Any Braille display for UTF-8 plaintext files and terminal screens needs to implement a transcription function that first maps all possible Unicode characters to sequences of Braille symbols before they can be displayed as Braille. Such a mapping function will likely try to follow common and domain-specific Braille acronyms as far as possible, and then use standard generic fallbacks for anything else, such as the character name from the Unicode database (e.g. `[BRAILLE PATTERN DOTS-125]`) or even just the hexadecimal code (e.g. `U+2813`). Therefore the Unicode Braille range will likely be treated in exactly the same way as any other rarely used Unicode character. 

There seems no demonstrated need, and nothing gained, by doing anything special here. Wether a user wants the 256 Unicode Braille symbols to be passed-through as they are, or displayed in a fall-back notation, will be up to how the Braille-terminal user configures their local mapping preferences table.

And regarding the (independent) question of what symbols should be allowed in Julia identifiers, I'd simply first consult [UTR #31](http://unicode.org/reports/tr31/tr31-2.html), the Unicode standard for use of Unicode characters in programming-language identifiers, and would hope for a Julia-specific justification for proposals to deviate from that in Julia.

The syntax (especially quoting of quotes) and even concepts (how does splitting the command-line into ARGS elements work and who does which part there) differ wildly between the Unix and Windows shells (and even between CMD.EXE and PowerShell!).

It sometimes can make more sense to provide both a POSIX shell *and* a CMD.EXE or a PowerShell prompt example, to raise awareness that there can be huge shell-specific differences when it comes to passing Julia syntax on the command-line. I think a better guideline would be to test all getting-started examples on
* bash 3 (for traditionalist macOS users still baulking at Apple's GPLv3 aversion)
* zsh (for newer macOS users)
* cmd.exe (for traditionalist Windows users)
* PowerShell 5.1 (for novelty-seeking Windows users)

and provide multiple examples in cases where a different syntax is more convenient.
